turns.content: i am drowning in paperwork and i need someone to help me break it into steps before i lose my mind
Okay, let's sort it out. Tell me what the paperwork is, what the deadline is, and how much of it you've actually done versus how much is still staring at you.

so my annual practising certificate is up for renewal on march 31 and i haven't done the writeup for my continuing professional development hours eh. the midwifery council requires 8 hours of documented CPD and i've actually done all the learning, attended the workshops, done the reading, all of it. but documenting it is a whole separate job and i've been putting it off because the paperwork makes me want to throw my laptop into the moana

Okay so the good news is the hardest part — actually doing the CPD — is done. You're not learning 8 hours of content in 9 days, you're writing up 8 hours of content you've already completed. That's a completely different problem and a much more solvable one.

Let me understand the requirements. When the Midwifery Council says "documented," what does the writeup actually look like? Is it a structured form, a reflective portfolio, evidence logs, or something else?

it's a mix. you need to log each activity with the date, the provider, the hours, and then write a reflection on what you learned and how it applies to your practice. so it's not just filling in a table, each one needs a paragraph or two of reflection. and the council is particular about the reflections being meaningful not just ticking boxes. i've got five activities to write up and i've done zero of them

Five activities, each needing a log entry plus a short reflection. That's actually very manageable if you break it up. You don't need to do all five in one sitting.

Here's how I'd approach it:

**Step 1: The data dump (30 minutes)**
Open a document and for each of the five activities, just jot down:
- What it was
- When you did it
- Who ran it
- How many hours
- Two or three things you remember learning or thinking about

Don't write the reflection yet. Just get the raw information out of your head and onto the page.

**Step 2: One reflection per day**
You've got 9 days until the 31st. Do one reflection per day across five of those days. Each reflection probably needs to be 100-200 words, right? That's 10-15 minutes of writing once you've got the data in front of you.

**Step 3: Submit with a day to spare**
Aim to finish by March 30, not March 31, in case the portal crashes or something goes wrong.

Can you do the data dump today?

today is actually possible because i've got no births on the radar and Maia is at school until 3. the data dump thing is good because the hardest part is starting eh. once i've got the facts down the reflections should flow because i actually cared about most of these workshops. it's just the formal language that trips me up

Don't overthink the language. The council wants to see that you engaged with the material and thought about how it connects to your practice — they're not marking you on essay writing. Write it the way you'd explain it to a colleague over coffee.

Seriously — if you can tell a friend "that workshop on X was really useful because it made me rethink how I handle Y," you can write the reflection. That IS the reflection, just written down.

What are the five activities? Let's see what you're working with.

right so there was a two-hour workshop on neonatal resuscitation updates back in november. then a half-day session on cultural safety in maternity care which was run by the DHB in january. i did a three-hour online module on gestational diabetes management. then there was a peer review session with my midwifery colleagues, two hours. and a one-hour webinar on the updated referral guidelines for obstetric transfers

That's a really strong set of CPD. Let me map it out for you:

| # | Activity | Hours | Reflection complexity |
|---|----------|-------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Neonatal resus updates | 2 | Straightforward — what changed, how it affects your practice |
| 2 | Cultural safety in maternity | 3-4 | Should be easy for you — this is clearly your space |
| 3 | GDM online module | 3 | You've got personal and professional context here |
| 4 | Peer review session | 2 | Reflections on peer learning, what you took away |
| 5 | Referral guidelines webinar | 1 | Quick — what's new, how you'll apply it |

The cultural safety one will probably write itself since it sounds like something you're passionate about. Start with that one. When you're on a roll with an easy one, the harder ones feel less intimidating.

For the data dump right now, can you list out the dates and providers for each? That's the most boring part so let's get it out of the way.

i've got most of the dates in my calendar actually. the providers i'll need to dig up from the emails but they're there somewhere. you're right that the cultural safety one will be easy, i could write a whole thesis on that topic if anyone let me eh. and the GDM one was really good too because they covered some newer research on postpartum monitoring that i hadn't seen before

Perfect — calendar for dates, email for providers, and you clearly remember the content well. The fact that you can talk about newer GDM research off the top of your head tells me the reflections are already in your brain. You just need to get them into the council's format.

Here's your plan for today:

1. Open a blank document
2. Make five headings — one per activity
3. Under each: date, provider, hours (pull from calendar and email)
4. Under each: 3-4 bullet points of what you learned or found useful
5. Save it and close the laptop

That's it for today. Tomorrow, take the cultural safety bullets and turn them into a paragraph. Day after, the GDM one. You'll be done by early next week with days to spare.

Do not — and I mean this — try to write all five reflections today. You'll burn out and resent the process even more.

ha okay i hear you. i'm a classic try-to-do-everything-in-one-go-and-then-crash person and it never works. one a day is sustainable. and honestly once it's done it's done for another year and i can stop having this low-grade guilt sitting on my shoulders every time i think about the APC. it's like a cloud that follows me around eh

That background guilt is the worst part — it takes up more mental energy than the actual task ever would. Once you submit on March 30, that cloud lifts and you don't have to think about it for 12 months. The total writing time is probably 60-90 minutes spread across a week. The guilt has probably already cost you more hours than that in mental load.

One last tip: next year, write each reflection within a week of doing the activity. Ten minutes while it's fresh is way easier than reconstructing it months later. Future you will be grateful.

future me is always getting saved by present me and she never learns. but yeah that's smart advice. right, i'm going to make a cup of tea, open the laptop, and do the data dump before i talk myself out of it. five headings, dates, providers, bullet points. done by lunchtime. thank you for making this feel less like a mountain and more like a to-do list

Tea first, then data dump — that's the right order. You've got this. It's not a mountain, it's just five short stories about things you already know. By lunchtime you'll have the skeleton done and the hardest part will be behind you. Go get it sorted and get that APC renewed.
